doc.title: A business's first week — three entries
doc.event: Rico opens a lawn-care company: he invests $5,000 cash; buys a $3,000 mower paying $1,000 down with the rest on a note; and finishes his first job for $400 cash. Journalize the week.

lines.label: asset in
lines.trap: Business ≠ Rico. Its books track ITS stuff.

lines.label: owner's claim
lines.trap: Never Service Revenue — nothing was earned.

lines.label: full cost of the asset
lines.why: The asset records at its FULL cost regardless of how it's financed.
lines.trap: Not 1,000 — how you PAY never changes what the thing COST.

lines.label: the down payment
lines.trap: A three-line (compound) entry is normal — debits (3,000) still equal credits (1,000 + 2,000).

lines.label: the borrowed rest
lines.why: A creditor's claim is born — a liability.
lines.trap: Borrowing is never revenue and repaying is never expense: loans move A and L, not income.

lines.label: earned AND collected
lines.why: This time cash and earning happen together.
lines.trap: Compare with billing a client (Ch. 2's third event): same revenue credit, different debit.

lines.label: equity grows by earning
lines.why: The first dollar the business ever EARNED.
lines.trap: Now equity has both doors open: 5,000 contributed + 400 earned.

computationPaths.narration: The equation after each day — always balanced:

memorize.body: Compound entries (3+ lines) are ordinary — the only law is total debits = total credits. Read the event, list what moved, balance it.
Assets record at FULL cost no matter the financing mix. The payment plan splits the CREDITS, never the debit.

questions.prompt: A $3,000 mower is bought with $1,000 cash and a $2,000 note. Equipment is debited for…

distractors.feedback: 1,000 is the CASH paid, not the asset's cost. Equipment debits for the full 3,000; the credits split between Cash (1,000) and Notes Payable (2,000).

build.scaffold: Three days, three entries. Day 2 is the teacher: full cost on the debit, financing mix on the credits.
